dish_name: Vietnamese Beef Noodle Soup (Phở Bò)
ingredients_notes: Broth base of beef bones (knuckle, marrow, oxtail) simmered 6–12 hours with charred onion, charred ginger, star anise, cinnamon, cloves, cardamom, coriander seeds, fish sauce, and rock sugar. Served over flat rice noodles (bánh phở) with thinly sliced rare beef (tái), optional cuts including brisket, tendon, or tripe. Garnished with bean sprouts, Thai basil, sawtooth herb, lime wedges, and fresh chilli. Condiments include hoisin sauce and sriracha served on the side.
dietary_considerations: High sodium: 1,100–1,400 mg per bowl, contributing approximately 48–60% of the recommended daily sodium intake of 2,300 mg; those with hypertension or kidney concerns should consume in moderation
Rice noodles are naturally gluten-free; however, fish sauce and hoisin sauce may contain trace gluten
Cross-contamination with gluten is possible in restaurant settings
Contains fish allergen via fish sauce
Hoisin sauce may contain soy and sesame allergens
Not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those avoiding beef or bone broth
High in complete protein; bone broth is rich in collagen peptides, glycine, and proline supporting joint and gut health
Good source of haem iron from beef, which is more bioavailable than plant-based iron
Rice noodles have a moderate glycaemic index of approximately 56–61, making this more suitable for blood sugar management than wheat-based noodle soups
Addition of hoisin sauce adds approximately 30–50 kcal and 200–300 mg sodium per serving
data_source_note: Estimated based on authentic Vietnamese preparation methods and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central, nutritional data from Vietnamese culinary sources, and established food composition databases. Actual values may vary by restaurant, recipe, and portion size.

dish_name: Vietnamese Chicken Noodle Soup (Phở Gà)
ingredients_notes: Bone-in chicken (~100–120g cooked per bowl), flat rice noodles (~80–100g dry weight), chicken bone broth (~450–500ml), charred ginger and onion, whole spices (star anise, cinnamon, cloves, cardamom, coriander seeds), fish sauce (~1–2 tbsp), bean sprouts, Thai basil, spring onion, fresh coriander, lime wedges, fresh chilli. Optional condiments: hoisin sauce, sriracha.
dietary_considerations: High sodium: one bowl contributes approximately 40–50% of the recommended daily sodium intake (2,000–2,300mg); primary sources are fish sauce and seasoned broth
Contains fish allergen: fish sauce is a core ingredient
May contain traces of shellfish depending on broth preparation
Gluten risk: rice noodles are naturally gluten-free but hoisin sauce typically contains wheat; cross-contamination possible in restaurant settings
Conditionally gluten-free: avoid hoisin sauce and confirm fish sauce brand is gluten-free
Dairy-free: typically dairy-free in authentic preparation
Low-fat friendly: relatively low in total and saturated fat when prepared with skin-off chicken
High-protein: provides approximately 28–32g of complete protein per bowl
Moderate-to-high glycaemic index due to rice noodles: portion control advised for diabetes management
Suitable for weight management: moderate calorie density with high satiety from protein and broth volume
Collagen-rich bone broth may support gut and joint health
data_source_note: Estimated based on authentic Vietnamese preparation methods. Nutritional values may vary depending on restaurant, recipe, and serving size.

dish_name: Banh Mi
ingredients_notes: Classic Banh Mi Thit Nguoi (cold cut combination) featuring a light airy baguette made with wheat and rice flour blend, Vietnamese cold cuts including cha lua (pork roll), pate, and sliced pork, pickled daikon and carrots (do chua), fresh cucumber, cilantro, jalapenos, and a spread of mayonnaise and/or butter. Reflects French colonial culinary influence on Vietnamese cuisine.
dietary_considerations: High sodium content - 950 to 1150mg per sandwich accounts for approximately 40 to 50 percent of the recommended daily sodium intake of 2300mg; caution advised for individuals with hypertension
Contains gluten - baguette is made with wheat flour, unsuitable for those with coeliac disease or gluten intolerance
Contains pork - not suitable for Halal or Kosher diets
Contains eggs via mayonnaise
May contain dairy if butter spread is used - request omission for dairy-free requirement
Contains soy via seasoning and sauces used in meat preparation
Vegetarian and vegan alternative available as Banh Mi Chay using tofu, mushrooms, and vegan pate
Pickled vegetables contribute beneficial probiotics and digestive enzymes
Relatively lower calorie density compared to Western sandwiches of similar size and weight
Provides a well-balanced macronutrient profile with moderate protein, complex carbohydrates, and fats
data_source_note: Estimated based on authentic Vietnamese preparation of Banh Mi Thit Nguoi. Values derived from USDA FoodData Central and FAO/INFOODS food composition databases. Actual nutritional values may vary depending on specific vendor, regional recipe variations, ingredient brands, and exact portion sizes.

dish_name: Goi Cuon (Vietnamese Fresh Spring Roll)
ingredients_notes: Each roll contains rice paper (banh trang), cooked rice vermicelli (bun), poached shrimp (2-3 halved pieces), sliced boiled pork (thit heo luoc), fresh lettuce, mint, mixed herbs, and bean sprouts. Typically served with a hoisin-peanut dipping sauce or nuoc cham (fish sauce-based dip), which are not included in the nutritional values above.
dietary_considerations: Contains shellfish (shrimp) — major allergen
Contains pork — not suitable for halal or kosher diets
Dipping sauce typically contains fish sauce — not suitable for vegetarians or vegans
Hoisin-peanut dipping sauce contains peanuts — major allergen
Rice paper is naturally gluten-free; however hoisin sauce in dipping sauce may contain wheat
Not suitable for vegetarian or vegan diets unless modified to remove shrimp and pork
Not suitable for low-carb or keto diets due to rice paper and vermicelli
Moderate sodium content (~180-220 mg per roll); dipping sauce will significantly increase sodium intake
Adding 2 tbsp hoisin-peanut dipping sauce adds approximately 80-100 kcal
Suitable for low-calorie, dairy-free, and heart-healthy diets
Moderate protein only — not ideal as a standalone high-protein meal
data_source_note: Estimated based on authentic Vietnamese preparation using standard ingredients. Nutritional values represent midpoint averages where ranges were provided. Values may vary depending on roll size, ingredient ratios, regional variations, and preparation method. Dipping sauce calories and macros are excluded.

dish_name: Cha Gio (Vietnamese Fried Spring Roll)
ingredients_notes: Rice paper wrapper (banh trang), ground pork, glass noodles (mien), wood ear mushrooms, shredded taro or carrot, shallots, garlic, optional shrimp or crab meat, seasoned with fish sauce (nuoc mam), black pepper and sugar. Deep-fried in vegetable oil at 175-180 degrees Celsius. Served with fresh lettuce, mint and nuoc cham dipping sauce. Dipping sauce nutrition values are not included in the above figures.
dietary_considerations: High sodium: fish sauce and seasoning contribute significant sodium; monitor intake if hypertensive
Contains shellfish allergen: many recipes include shrimp or crab; not suitable for shellfish allergies
Potential gluten presence: traditional rice paper wrappers are gluten-free but some commercial versions may use wheat wrappers; always verify
Contains fish allergen: fish sauce (nuoc mam) is a core ingredient; not suitable for fish allergies
Not vegetarian or vegan: contains pork, often shrimp, and fish sauce in both filling and dipping sauce
Deep-fried: moderate fat content from frying; those monitoring fat intake should be mindful of portion size
Moderate glycaemic impact: rice paper wrapper has a moderate GI; glass noodles have a relatively lower GI compared to wheat noodles
data_source_note: Estimated based on authentic Vietnamese preparation methods and standard nutritional databases including USDA and SEA Food Composition Tables. Actual values may vary depending on roll size, oil absorption during frying, and specific recipe variations.

dish_name: Bun Bo Hue
ingredients_notes: Broth base made from pork and beef bones slow-simmered with lemongrass, shrimp paste (mam ruoc), chilli oil, and fish sauce. Served with thick round rice vermicelli noodles (bun), beef shank, pork knuckle, pork blood cubes, and Vietnamese pork roll (cha lua). Garnished with bean sprouts, banana blossom, fresh herbs including mint and perilla, lime, and fresh chilli.
dietary_considerations: High sodium content: ~1,400–1,800mg per bowl, representing approximately 60–78% of the recommended daily intake of 2,300mg
Contains shellfish allergen: shrimp paste (mam ruoc) is a core broth ingredient
Contains fish allergen: fish sauce is commonly used in broth preparation
Possible gluten presence: some cha lua (pork roll) varieties may contain wheat starch — verify with vendor
Possible soy presence: certain condiments or garnishes may contain soy
Not suitable for Halal diets: contains pork, pork knuckle, and pork blood cubes
Not suitable for vegetarian or vegan diets: contains multiple animal proteins including beef, pork, and shrimp paste
Not recommended for low-sodium diets without significant modification
Suitable for dairy-free diets: naturally contains no dairy ingredients
Mostly suitable for gluten-free diets: verify cha lua and all condiments before consuming
High-protein dish: provides approximately 28–32g of protein per bowl
Notable collagen content from long-simmered bone broth
High iron content attributed to beef shank and pork blood cubes
To reduce calories and sodium: request less broth, omit pork blood cubes, and increase fresh herb garnishes
data_source_note: Estimated based on authentic Vietnamese preparation of Bun Bo Hue using a standard restaurant-style serving of approximately 150g noodles, 100g mixed meat, 300ml broth, and fresh garnishes. Nutritional values are approximations and may vary depending on specific recipe, preparation method, and portion size.

dish_name: Bun Cha
ingredients_notes: Grilled pork patties and pork belly served in a sweet-savory dipping broth (nuoc cham) made from fish sauce, vinegar, sugar, garlic, and chilli. Accompanied by rice vermicelli noodles and fresh herbs including lettuce, perilla, mint, and bean sprouts. Approximate composition per bowl: 120g grilled pork, 100g cooked rice vermicelli, 80ml dipping broth, 80g fresh herbs and vegetables.
dietary_considerations: High sodium — a single bowl accounts for approximately 60–70% of the recommended daily sodium intake of 2,000mg
Contains fish allergen — fish sauce (nuoc mam) is a primary ingredient
May contain gluten — some recipes include soy sauce or oyster sauce in the pork marinade
May contain peanuts — some restaurant versions garnish with crushed peanuts
Not suitable for gluten-free diets without confirming marinade ingredients
Not suitable for low-carb or ketogenic diets due to rice vermicelli noodles
Not suitable for paleo diets due to refined noodles and sugar in broth
Not suitable for pescatarians, vegetarians, or vegans — contains pork
Not suitable for halal diets — contains pork
Dairy-free
Sodium reduction tip: request dipping broth on the side and dilute with water or lime juice
data_source_note: Estimated based on authentic Vietnamese preparation of Bun Cha. Nutritional values are approximations and may vary depending on restaurant, portion size, and specific recipe variations. Micronutrient percent daily values are based on a 2,000 kcal daily diet.

dish_name: Com Tam (Vietnamese Broken Rice)
ingredients_notes: Classic Southern Vietnamese dish comprising broken jasmine rice, grilled pork chop (suon nuong), shredded pork skin (bi), steamed egg meatloaf (cha trung hap), fried egg, pickled daikon and carrot, sliced cucumber, spring onion oil, and nuoc cham fish sauce-based dipping sauce. Marinades may contain fish sauce, sugar, garlic, lemongrass, soy sauce, or oyster sauce depending on preparation.
dietary_considerations: High sodium content - single bowl accounts for approximately 50-65% of recommended daily sodium intake (2,000mg); caution advised for those with hypertension
High cholesterol - contributed by fried egg, pork chop, and steamed egg meatloaf combination
Contains fish and shellfish allergens via fish sauce (nuoc mam)
Contains eggs - fried egg and steamed egg meatloaf
Possible gluten presence - some marinades may include soy sauce or oyster sauce
Possible soy allergen - soy sauce occasionally used in pork marinade
Not suitable for Halal diets - pork is the primary protein
Not suitable for vegetarian or vegan diets
Not recommended for low-sodium diets without significant modification
Excellent for high-protein diets - approximately 40g protein per bowl
Moderate caution for diabetic diets - high refined carbohydrate load from broken jasmine rice
Conditionally gluten-free - requires verification of marinade and sauce ingredients
Dairy-free as typically prepared
To reduce sodium, request dipping sauce on the side and use sparingly
Requesting less spring onion oil can reduce fat content by approximately 30-40 kcal
data_source_note: Estimated based on authentic Vietnamese preparation of Com Tam as served in Southern Vietnam, particularly Ho Chi Minh City. Nutritional values are approximate midpoint estimates and may vary based on restaurant preparation, marinade sugar content, oil usage, and portion sizes.

dish_name: Banh Xeo Vietnamese Sizzling Crepe
ingredients_notes: Crepe batter made from rice flour, coconut milk, turmeric powder, water, and scallions. Filling includes pork belly, shrimp, mung bean sprouts, and green onions, cooked in coconut or vegetable oil. Served with fresh herbs including lettuce, mint, perilla, and Vietnamese coriander. Accompanied by nuoc cham dipping sauce made from fish sauce, lime juice, sugar, garlic, chilli, and water. Nutritional values include approximately 30ml of dipping sauce.
dietary_considerations: Contains shellfish — shrimp is a core ingredient; not suitable for shellfish allergies
Contains fish — fish sauce used in dipping sauce
Naturally gluten-free batter using rice flour; cross-contamination possible in restaurant settings
Contains coconut milk — relevant for those with tree nut or coconut sensitivities
Not suitable for pescatarians — contains pork
Not suitable for vegetarians or vegans — contains pork, shrimp, and fish sauce
High sodium content — primarily from fish sauce; intake varies with dipping sauce consumption
High in total fat and saturated fat due to generous oil use and coconut milk
Not suitable for keto or low-carb diets — rice flour batter is carbohydrate-heavy
Vitamin C sourced primarily from fresh bean sprouts and accompanying herbs
Iron sourced primarily from pork and shrimp
Wrapping crepe in fresh lettuce and herbs adds negligible calories but boosts micronutrient and antioxidant value
Regional size and richness variations exist between Southern and Central Vietnamese preparations
data_source_note: Estimated based on authentic Vietnamese home-style and street-food preparation methods. Values may vary depending on crepe size, oil quantity, filling ratios, dipping sauce volume consumed, and regional recipe variations.

dish_name: Cao Lau
ingredients_notes: Thick chewy rice-and-ash noodles (yellow-green hue from wood ash lye water), char siu-style sliced pork, crispy pork crackling (top mo), fresh herbs (mint, Vietnamese coriander, lettuce), bean sprouts, and a small amount of concentrated soy-based broth. Pork is marinated in soy sauce. Dish is notably drier than most Vietnamese noodle soups.
dietary_considerations: High sodium: 900-1100mg per plate (~38-48% of recommended daily intake); not ideal for those managing hypertension or cardiovascular conditions
Contains gluten: soy sauce in pork marinade and broth contains wheat; not suitable for Coeliac disease without verified gluten-free soy sauce substitution
Contains soy: present in marinade and broth
Contains pork: not suitable for Halal or Kosher diets, or those avoiding pork for religious or dietary reasons
Not suitable for vegetarian or vegan diets
Not suitable for gluten-free diets unless modified
Dairy-free: suitable
Nut-free: suitable
Good high-protein option: 28-32g protein per plate
Crispy pork crackling is the primary contributor to saturated fat; requesting less crackling can reduce fat content
No shellfish or seafood in traditional preparation
data_source_note: Estimated based on authentic Vietnamese preparation methods specific to Hoi An. Nutritional values may vary depending on restaurant portioning, marinade recipes, and crackling quantity.

dish_name: Mi Quang
ingredients_notes: Wide turmeric-yellow rice noodles served with a small amount of rich turmeric-infused pork bone and shrimp paste broth. Toppings include pork, shrimp, quail eggs, crushed peanuts, and sesame rice crackers (banh trang). Garnished generously with fresh herbs and vegetables including banana blossom, lettuce, bean sprouts, mint, and perilla. Finished with annatto-infused cooking oil.
dietary_considerations: High sodium content: 900–1200mg per plate, representing approximately 39–52% of the recommended daily limit of 2300mg
Contains shellfish: shrimp is a core ingredient
Contains peanuts: crushed peanuts are an integral topping and cannot easily be omitted
Contains eggs: quail eggs are a standard component
Possible gluten presence: sesame rice crackers and shrimp paste may contain wheat or gluten-containing ingredients
Possible soy presence: some preparations use soy-based condiments
Not suitable for vegetarian or vegan diets due to pork, shrimp, and eggs
Not suitable for nut-free diets due to peanuts
Nutrient-dense despite moderate calorie count, offering strong protein, complex carbohydrates, and micronutrients
Carbohydrates are primarily from rice noodles and sesame crackers, providing slow-to-moderate glycaemic index sources
Fats are primarily derived from annatto-infused cooking oil, pork, and peanuts
Sodium and calorie counts will increase if fish sauce, chilli, or extra crackers are added at the table
Vitamin C and Vitamin A are notably boosted by the fresh herb and vegetable garnishes
Iron content is enhanced by peanuts and the turmeric-annatto broth base
data_source_note: Estimated based on authentic Vietnamese preparation methods using traditional ingredients. Values derived from USDA food composition database and Vietnamese food composition data. Actual values may vary depending on restaurant, home cook, regional variation, and specific ingredient quantities used.

dish_name: Banh Cuon Vietnamese Steamed Rice Roll
ingredients_notes: Thin steamed rice flour wrapper made from fermented rice batter, filled with seasoned lean ground pork, wood ear mushrooms, and sauteed shallots. Lightly greased with neutral cooking oil during steaming. Fish sauce used as seasoning in filling. Traditionally served with cha lua (Vietnamese pork sausage), crispy fried shallots, bean sprouts, fresh herbs, and nuoc cham dipping sauce, which are excluded from the nutritional values above.
dietary_considerations: Contains fish sauce - not suitable for fish or shellfish allergies
Contains pork - not suitable for Halal, Kosher, vegetarian, or vegan diets
Moderate sodium at 320mg per roll before dipping sauce - a full serving of 3 to 4 rolls with nuoc cham can exceed 900 to 1200mg sodium
Gluten-free if prepared with pure rice flour and gluten-free fish sauce, though cross-contamination is possible in restaurant settings
Low in dietary fiber due to refined rice flour wrapper
Relatively low calorie compared to many other Vietnamese dishes
Low in saturated fat due to lean pork filling
Dairy-free
Suitable for pescatarian diets given fish sauce usage
Not suitable for vegetarian or vegan diets
data_source_note: Estimated based on authentic Vietnamese preparation using USDA FoodData Central, FAO food composition tables, and Vietnamese culinary nutritional references. Values may vary based on regional preparation and restaurant-specific recipes.

dish_name: Hu Tieu (Hủ Tiếu Nam Vang)
ingredients_notes: Clear rice noodles in a light, sweet pork-and-dried-shrimp broth, topped with sliced pork, minced pork, shrimp, quail eggs, and fresh herbs including bean sprouts. Seasoned with fish sauce and dried shrimp. Based on the classic Hủ Tiếu Nam Vang (Phnom Penh-style) wet preparation.
dietary_considerations: High sodium content: 1,100–1,400 mg per bowl (48–61% of recommended daily intake); caution advised for those managing hypertension or kidney conditions
Contains shellfish: shrimp and dried shrimp present
Contains eggs: quail eggs present
Contains fish: fish sauce used in broth and seasoning
Possible gluten: soy sauce or wheat-based noodle variants may be used; confirm rice noodles for gluten-free requirement
Possible soy: present in condiments and sauces
Not suitable for vegetarian or vegan diets: contains pork, shrimp, eggs, and fish sauce

Not suitable for paleo diets: processed condiments present

Dry version (khô) may allow better sodium control by limiting broth consumption

dish_name: Bun Rieu
ingredients_notes: Rice vermicelli noodles in a tomato-based broth enriched with crab paste (mam cua) and pork. Typical toppings include crab and pork meatballs, tofu, tomatoes, shrimp paste (mam tom), bean sprouts, banana blossom, perilla leaves, and lime. Fish sauce is used for seasoning.
dietary_considerations: Contains shellfish: crab paste and shrimp paste are major allergens
Contains soy: tofu is a standard ingredient
May contain gluten: some fermented shrimp pastes or sauces may contain wheat; verify before serving to gluten-sensitive individuals
Rice noodles are naturally gluten-free
High sodium content: 1,400-1,800 mg per bowl, representing 60-78% of the recommended daily intake of 2,300 mg
Not suitable for vegetarians or vegans due to crab paste, shrimp paste, and pork
Not suitable for pescatarians due to pork content
Not recommended for low-sodium diets without modification
Dairy-free
Nut-free
Good high-protein option
Tomato-rich broth provides lycopene, a beneficial antioxidant

dish_name: Banh Canh (Vietnamese Thick Noodle Soup)
ingredients_notes: Thick tapioca or rice-flour noodles served in a rich pork bone or seafood broth seasoned with fish sauce, shallots, and black pepper. Common toppings include shrimp, sliced pork, fish cake (cha ca), spring onions, and fried shallots. This estimate is based on the standard Banh Canh Tom/Thit (shrimp and pork) variation.
dietary_considerations: HIGH SODIUM: 1,100-1,400mg per bowl, representing 48-61% of the recommended daily intake of 2,300mg
Contains shellfish: shrimp and/or crab are common toppings
Contains fish: fish sauce used in broth; fish cake commonly included as topping
Possible gluten: some fish cakes or broths may contain wheat; tapioca noodles are naturally gluten-free but cross-contamination risk exists
Possible soy: some versions may use soy-based condiments
Not suitable for vegetarians or vegans: contains meat, seafood, and fish sauce
Not suitable for low-sodium diets without modification
Halal status uncertain: pork is commonly used in preparation
Dairy-free: no dairy ingredients present
Nut-free: no nut ingredients present
Good source of protein for high-protein diets
Pork knuckle version (Banh Canh Gio Heo) is notably higher in fat (18-22g) and calories (500-550 kcal)
Reducing broth consumption can lower sodium intake by approximately 30-40%
Adding fresh herbs such as bean sprouts, lime, and chilli boosts Vitamin C and antioxidants with minimal caloric impact
data_source_note: Estimated based on authentic Vietnamese preparation using standard Banh Canh Tom/Thit (shrimp and pork) recipe. Values derived from USDA, FAO, and Vietnamese food nutrition reference databases. Actual values may vary depending on restaurant preparation, portion size, and specific ingredients used.

dish_name: Com Ga Hoi An - Vietnamese Hội An Chicken Rice
ingredients_notes: Free-range poached or simmered chicken hand-shredded and served over turmeric-infused rice cooked in chicken broth. Topped with crispy shallots, Vietnamese coriander (rau ram), spring onions, toasted sesame seeds, and optional mint. Served with a tangy-sweet-spicy dipping sauce of chilli, lime juice, fish sauce (nuoc mam), garlic, and sugar. Accompanied by pickled papaya or daikon on the side. Key aromatics include lemongrass, ginger, shallots, and turmeric.
dietary_considerations: High sodium content due to fish sauce and seasoned broth — individuals managing hypertension or following a low-sodium diet should exercise caution
Contains fish allergen — fish sauce (nuoc mam) is a core ingredient; not suitable for those with fish or shellfish allergies unless substituted
Generally low-gluten or gluten-free if no soy sauce is used — verify with restaurant as some versions may include soy-based condiments
Not suitable for vegetarians or vegans — contains chicken and fish sauce
Moderate to high carbohydrate content from turmeric-infused rice — not ideal for low-carb or ketogenic diets
High protein content from lean poached and shredded chicken — supports satiety and muscle maintenance
Contains anti-inflammatory ingredients — turmeric (curcumin) and ginger offer notable anti-inflammatory properties
Relatively moderate in fat and calories compared to fried rice or heavily sauced dishes
data_source_note: Estimated based on authentic Vietnamese preparation of Com Ga Hoi An. Actual nutritional values may vary depending on restaurant, portion size, oil usage, and specific recipe variations.

dish_name: Bo Luc Lac (Vietnamese Shaking Beef)
ingredients_notes: Primary ingredients include cubed sirloin, ribeye, or tenderloin beef marinated in oyster sauce, fish sauce, soy sauce, sugar, garlic, sesame oil, and black pepper. Served on a salad base of watercress or butter lettuce with sliced tomatoes and red onion. Accompanied by a lime juice, salt, black pepper, and chilli dipping sauce. Cooked in neutral oil at high heat. Optionally served with steamed jasmine rice (not included in calorie estimate) or Vietnamese baguette.
dietary_considerations: High sodium: ~980mg per bowl, approximately 43% of the recommended daily intake of 2300mg — those managing hypertension should be mindful
Contains gluten: soy sauce and oyster sauce typically contain wheat — not suitable for coeliac disease unless gluten-free alternatives are used
Contains fish allergen: fish sauce is a core ingredient — not suitable for fish or seafood allergies
Contains shellfish allergen: oyster sauce contains oyster extract — not suitable for shellfish allergies
Not vegetarian or vegan: contains beef, fish sauce, and oyster sauce
Diabetic awareness: moderate sugar content from marinade; glycaemic impact is relatively low without rice
High protein: excellent source of complete protein — suitable for high-protein or low-carb diets when served without rice
Potentially keto-adaptable: reduce sugar in marinade and omit rice to lower net carbohydrates to approximately 8–10g
data_source_note: Estimated based on authentic Vietnamese preparation methods using standard restaurant-style portioning. Actual values may vary depending on the cut of beef used, marinade quantities, and whether the dish is served with rice or bread. Rice side not included in calorie estimate; adding 1 cup of steamed jasmine rice adds approximately 205 kcal and 45g carbohydrates.

dish_name: Cha Ca La Vong Vietnamese Turmeric Fish with Dill
portion_size: 1 bowl (approximately 350-400g, including fish, herbs, noodles and accompaniments)
ingredients_notes: Primary ingredients include turmeric-marinated catfish or snakehead fish (150-170g) providing protein, omega-3 fatty acids, vitamin B12 and vitamin D; cooked bun rice vermicelli noodles (80-100g) as the carbohydrate base; cooking oil (15-20ml) for frying; fresh dill and spring onions (20-30g) contributing vitamin C and antioxidants; roasted peanuts (15-20g) adding healthy fats, protein and magnesium; fermented shrimp paste mam tom (10-15g) for umami and sodium; turmeric marinade with fish sauce and galangal (15-20ml) providing curcumin and sodium; and fresh herbs including perilla and mint (10-15g) supplying antioxidants and vitamin K.
dietary_considerations: Contains fish - primary protein source; not suitable for fish allergies
Contains shellfish - fermented shrimp paste (mam tom) is a core condiment and a significant allergen risk
Contains peanuts - roasted peanuts are a standard topping and a major allergen

Not vegetarian or vegan - contains fish and shrimp-based condiments
Not suitable for pescatarians avoiding shellfish due to shrimp paste
Not suitable for keto or low-carb diets in standard form due to rice noodles; can be modified by omitting noodles
Generally gluten-free when prepared traditionally with rice vermicelli; verify fish sauce and shrimp paste brands for gluten-containing additives
High in lean protein - excellent source for muscle maintenance and satiety
Rich in omega-3 fatty acids from catfish and snakehead fish supporting heart health
Anti-inflammatory properties from turmeric curcumin and fresh dill
Vitamin B12 content meets approximately 115-145% of the recommended daily intake
data_source_note: Estimated based on authentic Vietnamese preparation of Cha Ca La Vong. Nutritional values represent midpoint averages of stated ranges and may vary depending on restaurant, portion size, and ingredient substitutions.

dish_name: Banh Beo Vietnamese Steamed Rice Cakes
ingredients_notes: Base made from rice flour and tapioca starch providing primary carbohydrates. Topped with dried or fresh shrimp contributing protein, iodine, and cholesterol. Crispy pork rinds (top mo) add saturated fat and protein. Scallion oil (mo hanh) drizzled for flavour contributing fat and Vitamin K. Served with a fish sauce-based dipping sauce (nuoc cham) adding sodium and trace sugars.
dietary_considerations: Contains shellfish - shrimp is a core ingredient, not suitable for shellfish allergies
Contains fish - fish sauce (nuoc mam) used in dipping sauce

Those on low-sodium diets should request reduced fish sauce or dipping sauce on the side
A typical full serving of 8-10 cakes provides approximately 415-580 kcal and 1,500-2,000mg sodium

dish_name: Banh Khot Vietnamese Crispy Mini Savoury Pancake
ingredients_notes: Batter made from rice flour, tapioca starch, coconut milk, turmeric powder, water, and salt. Topped with one small shrimp marinated in fish sauce and garlic. Cooked in coconut oil or vegetable oil. Optional garnishes include mung bean paste and crispy dried shrimp flakes. Served with fresh herbs (lettuce, mint, perilla, bean sprouts) and nuoc cham dipping sauce (fish sauce, lime juice, sugar, chilli, garlic). Dipping sauce and fresh herbs not included in nutritional values; adding 1 tbsp nuoc cham adds approximately 15-20 kcal and 300-400 mg additional sodium.

Contains fish derivatives (fish sauce) - not suitable for fish allergies
Typically gluten-free (rice and tapioca flour base) - verify no wheat starch is added
Dairy-free - coconut milk used instead of dairy
Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians - contains shrimp and fish sauce
Moderate sodium per piece - sodium accumulates quickly when eating multiple pancakes with dipping sauce
Relatively higher saturated fat due to coconut milk and coconut oil - mindful consumption advised for those monitoring cardiovascular health
Moderate-to-high glycaemic index due to rice flour base - relevant for those managing blood sugar
Typical restaurant serving of 6-10 pieces totals approximately 410-750 kcal before dipping sauce
data_source_note: Estimated based on authentic Vietnamese preparation methods. Values may vary slightly depending on shrimp size, oil quantity used, and regional recipe variations.

dish_name: Nem Nuong Grilled Pork Skewer Pancake
ingredients_notes: Key components include one grilled seasoned pork skewer (nem nuong, ~60g) providing protein, fat, and B vitamins; banh hoi or rice vermicelli sheet (~40g) as the primary carbohydrate source; rice paper banh trang (~10g); fresh herbs such as mint, perilla, and cilantro (~15g) contributing Vitamin C and antioxidants; lettuce and cucumber (~20g) for fiber and hydration; pickled daikon and carrot do chua (~15g) adding sodium and trace Vitamin C; peanut-hoisin dipping sauce (~20g) contributing fat, sugar, and sodium; and green onion oil mo hanh (~5g) adding additional fat.
dietary_considerations: High sodium content (~620mg per pancake) — caution advised for individuals with hypertension or kidney concerns
Contains gluten — hoisin sauce typically contains wheat; not suitable for coeliac disease or gluten intolerance without gluten-free substitution
Contains peanuts — present in the dipping sauce; risk for peanut allergy sufferers
Contains fish and shellfish derivatives — fish sauce (nuoc mam) is a core ingredient
Contains soy — present in hoisin sauce
Not suitable for vegetarians or vegans — contains pork and fish sauce
Not suitable for strict low-sodium diets without significant modification
Dairy-free — suitable for lactose-intolerant individuals
Gluten-free adaptation possible with gluten-free hoisin sauce substitution
Suitable for high-protein and moderate-carbohydrate dietary approaches
Lower fat option available by reducing dipping sauce and omitting green onion oil, saving approximately 40–50 kcal per serving

dish_name: Thit Kho Tau Vietnamese Braised Pork & Eggs
portion_size: 1 plate (approx. 300g — 2 pieces pork belly + 1–2 eggs + braising sauce)
ingredients_notes: Primary ingredients include pork belly (~150g) as the main protein and fat source, 1–2 whole eggs providing protein and cholesterol, coconut water (~60ml) used as a braising liquid and natural sweetener, fish sauce (~2 tbsp) contributing significant sodium and umami, palm or rock sugar (~1.5 tbsp) for caramelisation and sweetness, and aromatics including shallots, garlic, and black pepper for flavour.
dietary_considerations: High sodium: ~980–1,100 mg per serving, approximately 43–48% of the recommended daily intake of 2,300 mg

High saturated fat: primarily from pork belly
Not suitable for Halal diets — contains pork
Not suitable for Kosher diets — contains pork and mixes meat with eggs
Not suitable for vegetarian or vegan diets — contains pork, eggs, and fish sauce
Caution for low-sodium diets due to high fish sauce content
Caution for low-fat diets due to high fat content from pork belly
Moderate suitability for keto or low-carb diets — contains ~18g carbohydrates with notable sugar content
Generally gluten-free, but fish sauce brand should be verified as some varieties contain wheat
Dairy-free — no dairy ingredients used
Suitable for high-protein diets — provides ~32g protein per serving
If served with 1 cup of steamed jasmine rice, add approximately 205 kcal, 4g protein, and 45g carbohydrates

dish_name: Ca Kho To Vietnamese Caramelised Fish in Clay Pot
ingredients_notes: Primary protein is catfish or snakehead fish (ca tra or ca loc), rich in omega-3 fatty acids. Key flavouring components include caramel sauce (nuoc mau) made from palm or white sugar, fish sauce (nuoc mam) contributing significant sodium and umami, shallots, garlic, fresh chilli, coconut water (used in southern Vietnamese versions for natural sweetness and tenderising), black pepper, and spring onions as garnish.
dietary_considerations: High sodium — approximately 1,450mg per serving, representing 60–65% of the recommended daily intake of 2,300mg; caution advised for individuals managing hypertension, kidney disease, or cardiovascular conditions
Generally gluten-free — verify fish sauce brand as some varieties contain wheat
Dairy-free
Nut-free
Shellfish-free — contains fish only
Suitable for pescatarians
Not suitable for vegetarians or vegans
Moderate carbohydrate content — added sugars from caramelisation process may not be suitable for strict low-carb or ketogenic diets
Diabetic consideration — contains approximately 15g added sugars from caramelisation and coconut water; glycaemic intake monitoring recommended
High protein — excellent protein source at approximately 32g per serving
When served with a standard portion of steamed jasmine rice, total meal calories reach approximately 580–600 kcal
data_source_note: Estimated based on authentic Vietnamese preparation methods and standard ingredient quantities for traditional Ca Kho To. Actual values may vary depending on the restaurant, home cook, fish species used, and regional recipe variations such as northern versus southern Vietnamese style.

dish_name: Goi Ga Vietnamese Chicken Salad
ingredients_notes: Poached or rotisserie shredded chicken, finely shredded cabbage, fresh herbs (Vietnamese mint, perilla, cilantro), thinly sliced onion, shredded carrots, toasted peanuts, fried shallots, and prawn crackers. Dressed with a nuoc cham-style vinaigrette made from fish sauce, lime juice, sugar, garlic, and chilli. Nutritional values include prawn crackers; without crackers, calories reduce by approximately 60-80 kcal and carbohydrates by approximately 8g.
dietary_considerations: Contains fish allergen: fish sauce (nuoc mam)
Contains shellfish allergen: prawn crackers if included
Contains peanuts: toasted peanut garnish - must be explicitly requested to be omitted for allergy sufferers
Possible gluten content: some prawn crackers may contain gluten - verify labelling
No dairy used in authentic preparation

Not suitable for vegetarians or vegans: contains chicken and fish sauce
Mostly gluten-free but fish sauce and crackers should be verified as certified gluten-free
Moderate suitability for low-carb or keto diets: dressing contains sugar, request less sugar to reduce carbohydrate content

Fresh herbs including mint, perilla, and cilantro contribute antioxidants and anti-inflammatory compounds not fully captured in standard nutritional databases

dish_name: Lau Thai Thai-Style Vietnamese Hot Pot
ingredients_notes: Broth base includes lemongrass, galangal, kaffir lime leaves, bird's eye chillies, tamarind paste, tomatoes, pineapple, and fish sauce. Proteins typically include prawns, squid, fish fillets (snakehead or tilapia), and blood cockles, with optional thinly sliced beef or pork. Vegetables include enoki mushrooms, straw mushrooms, water spinach, taro stem, tomatoes, and bean sprouts. Served with rice vermicelli noodles, fresh herbs (Thai basil, Vietnamese coriander), and lime wedges.
dietary_considerations: Contains shellfish allergens: prawns, cockles, squid
Contains fish allergens: fish sauce, fish fillets
May contain gluten: some commercial broth pastes may include wheat
High sodium: one bowl accounts for approximately 45–57% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2,300mg
High spice level due to bird's eye chillies; adjustable upon request
Not suitable for vegetarians or vegans in standard form; adaptable with tofu, mushrooms, and soy-based seasoning
Can be made gluten-free if rice vermicelli is used and fish sauce or broth paste is verified gluten-free
High protein: excellent source of lean protein from mixed seafood
Heart health consideration: rich in omega-3 fatty acids from seafood, but high sodium warrants moderation

dish_name: Bun Thit Nuong Vietnamese Grilled Pork Rice Vermicelli
ingredients_notes: Key ingredients include cooked rice vermicelli noodles, chargrilled lemongrass-marinated pork, fresh herbs, shredded lettuce, bean sprouts, pickled daikon and carrots, crushed roasted peanuts, crispy fried shallots, and Nuoc Cham dipping sauce made from fish sauce, lime juice, sugar, garlic, and chilli. Some versions include a fried spring roll (cha gio) as an optional topping.
dietary_considerations: High sodium content (~1,100-1,400mg per bowl) primarily from Nuoc Cham dipping sauce and fish sauce marinade — individuals managing hypertension should use sauce sparingly or request it on the side
Contains fish allergen — fish sauce is used in both the dipping sauce and pork marinade; not suitable for those with fish or seafood allergies
Contains peanuts — crushed roasted peanuts are a standard topping; significant peanut allergen risk

Lower in saturated fat compared to heavier noodle soups due to grilling preparation method

dish_name: Banh Trang Nuong Grilled Rice Paper / Vietnamese Pizza
ingredients_notes: Thin dried rice paper base grilled over charcoal or flame, topped with beaten egg, green onions, dried shrimp or pork floss (ruoc), quail eggs, mayonnaise, chili sauce. Optional toppings may include processed cheese or sausage depending on vendor.

Contains eggs - present in both the base mixture and as quail eggs

dish_name: Xoi Xeo Vietnamese Mung Bean Sticky Rice
ingredients_notes: Primary ingredients include steamed glutinous rice (150–160g) as the main carbohydrate source, cooked yellow mung beans (50–60g) providing protein, fiber, folate and iron, vegetable oil or lard (10–15ml) contributing to fat content and caloric density, fried shallots (10–15g) for flavour, turmeric powder (1–2g) for colour and anti-inflammatory curcumin, and salt or seasoning (0.5–1g) as a sodium contributor. Some versions may include dried shredded coconut or chả lụa (Vietnamese pork sausage), which increases protein by 5–7g, sodium by 150–200mg, and calories by 60–80 kcal.
dietary_considerations: High in carbohydrates — glutinous rice has a high glycaemic index (GI ~86–98); not recommended for diabetics or those managing blood sugar without portion control
Moderate to high sodium — street-food versions may contain 500–600mg sodium; caution advised for individuals on low-sodium diets
Calorie-dense due to combination of sticky rice and cooking oil

dish_name: Chao Ga Vietnamese Chicken Rice Porridge
ingredients_notes: Key ingredients include jasmine or broken rice slow-cooked to a silky texture, whole chicken or chicken pieces poached in broth, homemade chicken stock with ginger and shallots, fresh ginger, fish sauce (nuoc mam) as primary seasoning, shallots and garlic. Garnished with fresh Vietnamese coriander (rau ram), spring onions, crispy fried shallots, white pepper, and lime wedges. Optional additions include century egg, mushrooms, or chilli.

dish_name: Banh Pia Vietnamese Mooncake Pastry
ingredients_notes: Multi-layered flaky pastry shell made from wheat flour, lard or vegetable shortening, and sugar using a laminated dough technique. Primary filling is sweetened mung bean paste (dau xanh). Common additions include salted egg yolk (trung muoi), durian paste (sau rieng), taro paste, or lotus seed paste. Flavored with pandan extract, sesame oil, and vanilla. Nutritional values reflect the classic mung bean paste and salted egg yolk version. Durian-filled versions may contain approximately 3-5g additional fat and 20-30 additional kcal.
dietary_considerations: Contains gluten (wheat flour) — not suitable for celiac or gluten-intolerant individuals

dish_name: Bun Mam Vietnamese Fermented Fish Noodle Soup
ingredients_notes: Key ingredients include fermented fish paste (mam ca linh or mam ca sac), thick rice vermicelli noodles (bun), pork belly or pork ribs, fresh or dried shrimp, squid, eggplant, lemongrass, shallots, garlic, chilli, and optional coconut milk. Garnished with bean sprouts, water spinach, banana blossom, Vietnamese mint, and perilla. Seasoned with fish sauce, sugar, and MSG.

dish_name: Com Chien Duong Chau Vietnamese Yangzhou Fried Rice
ingredients_notes: Day-old jasmine rice, eggs scrambled into rice, small peeled shrimp or prawns, char siu Chinese BBQ pork or ham or lap cheong Chinese sausage, carrots, green peas, corn kernels, spring onions or scallions, garlic, shallots. Seasoned with fish sauce, light soy sauce, oyster sauce, sesame oil, and white pepper. Cooked in vegetable oil or lard in traditional versions.

dish_name: Ga Nuong Sa Te Vietnamese Satay Grilled Chicken
ingredients_notes: Bone-in or boneless chicken thighs or drumsticks marinated in finely minced fresh lemongrass, sa te chilli paste (containing chilli, lemongrass, shrimp paste, and oil), garlic, shallots, fish sauce (nuoc mam), sugar or honey for caramelisation, vegetable or coconut oil, dried chilli flakes, and optionally turmeric powder. Commonly served with steamed jasmine rice or vermicelli noodles, fresh herbs, pickled vegetables, and nuoc cham dipping sauce.

Served with mam nem fermented anchovy dipping sauce and fish sauce (nuoc mam) seasoning.

dish_name: Che Ba Mau Vietnamese Three-Colour Dessert
